codeReviewIssues.description: EARLIEST_DEADLINE sentinel value 0 means 'no timers'. If a timer's deadline were exactly counter tick 0, it would be treated as 'no timers'. Practically impossible (counter starts well above 0 by the time userspace runs), but the sentinel conflates a valid value with the empty state. A dedicated AtomicBool or Option-encoding would be cleaner.

Source 2 (replenishment scan) includes all contexts with has_budget() && replenish_at > now. This means a context that was just replenished and has full budget still contributes its (far-future) replenish_at as a deadline. Benign — it's dominated by the shorter quantum deadline — but scanning all contexts for a deadline that won't matter adds minor overhead. A filter for only exhausted-budget contexts would be more precise.
.map(|t| scheduler_deadline_ticks(t, ...)).flatten() could be .and_then(|t| scheduler_deadline_ticks(t, ...)) — idiomatic Rust and avoids a Clippy lint (clippy::map_flatten).

timer::create() skips reprogram_next_deadline for timers < 100µs in the future. If the creating core is about to go idle (no other deadlines), the short timer would only fire when the u32::MAX long-sleep expires (~68s). In practice this can't happen (timer_create is a syscall from a running user thread, and the next schedule_inner will reprogram), but the rationale should be documented in the comment: 'safe because timer_create returns to a running thread that will schedule before going idle'.

check_expired() only calls update_earliest_deadline_locked when wake_count > 0. If all expired timers have no registered waiters (notify returns None), the cache is not updated even though those timers are still occupied slots with past deadlines. Not a bug (past deadlines cause 'fire immediately' on next reprogram), but causes an unnecessary immediate-fire cycle on each schedule_inner until the timer is destroyed or polled.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md says 'reprogram_next_deadline(core_id)' but the actual implementation signature is reprogram_next_deadline(scheduler_deadline_ticks: Option<u64>) — no core_id parameter. The function implicitly operates on the calling core via per-core hardware registers (CNTPCT, CNTP_TVAL). AGENTS.md was written at spec time and should be updated to match the final API.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: AGENTS.md line: '`reprogram_next_deadline(core_id)` must consider ALL deadline sources' vs timer.rs:134: 'pub fn reprogram_next_deadline(scheduler_deadline_ticks: Option<u64>)'

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker's skillFeedback suggests adding lock ordering analysis guidance to the os-worker skill: 'The os-worker skill could mention that lock ordering analysis should be done before any change that acquires locks from new call sites, especially when crossing module boundaries (scheduler → timer)'. This is a good observation — the worker discovered a STATE→TIMERS ordering edge during implementation that required a design change (switching to AtomicU64 cache). Pre-implementation lock ordering analysis could catch these earlier.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff skillFeedback.suggestedChanges[0]. Worker discovered the issue during implementation, fixed it by switching to lock-free AtomicU64, and documented it in LOCK-ORDERING.md.

summary: Solid implementation of tickless idle. The fixed 250Hz tick is completely removed (TICKS_PER_SEC, TICKS counter, freq/250 logic all gone). reprogram_next_deadline correctly computes the minimum deadline across timer objects (via lock-free AtomicU64 cache), scheduler quantum expiry, and context replenishment. Called from all required sites: schedule_inner (covers timer IRQ → schedule path, idle thread selection, and new thread selection), and timer::create (for future timers > 100µs). Secondary core init programs u32::MAX ticks (no fixed interval). Lock ordering preserved via the clever AtomicU64 cache that avoids acquiring TIMERS from inside STATE. All unsafe blocks have correct SAFETY comments; nomem correctly omitted from CNTP_TVAL writes. 17 model-based tests cover all deadline computation paths. 1768 tests pass including stress (72s) and integration (display pipeline). No blocking issues found.
